text:
Given its numerous applications, the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem remains a widely studied NP-hard problem. In the recent years, branch-and-reduce algorithms have become the fastest exact algorithms on real-world instances. The design of efficient reduction rules has played a crucial role in recent advances, while other components have been somewhat neglected. In particular, while selecting a vertex of high degree has been the default branching strategy for decades, there is little understanding of the performance of this choice, and there is no clear argument to support it.

We present a new approach by employing a branching strategy steered by a Graph Neural Network (GNN). Due to the inherent intricacy of the state space of today’s solver implementations, it is generally not feasible to apply either supervised or reinforcement learning. We therefore make use of a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s weights towards small values, which are associated with higher solver performance. Experimental results on a set of challenging benchmark instances confirm the feasibility of learning-based solver technologies. The speedup achieved on 73% of our benchmarks ranges from a factor of 2.4 up to 25.4 with a median value of 24.
